name: code-of-lipit-ishtar
description: A Sumerian king issued a law code in the Akkadian language, predating Hammurabi by over a century.

name: laws-of-eshnunna
description: This Babylonian-era code set fixed prices and penalties, an early Mesopotamian legal milestone.

name: hittite-law-code
description: The Hittite empire compiled around 200 laws favoring fines and restitution over harsh physical punishment.

name: mosaic-law-given
description: Hebrew tradition holds that Moses received the Ten Commandments at Mount Sinai, shaping Western moral law.

name: gortyn-law-code
description: Carved on a stone wall in Crete, this is the most complete surviving ancient Greek legal code.

name: solon-reforms-athens
description: Solon cancelled debts and rewrote Athens' harsh laws, laying foundations for later Greek democracy.

name: trial-of-socrates
description: An Athenian jury condemned the philosopher Socrates for impiety, sentencing him to drink hemlock.

name: lex-canuleia
description: This Roman law legalized marriage between patricians and plebeians, easing class tensions in the republic.

name: lex-villia-annalis
description: Rome set minimum ages for holding public office, regulating the political career ladder of magistrates.

name: lex-aquilia
description: This Roman statute established liability for wrongful damage to property, shaping later tort law.

name: praetor-peregrinus-created
description: Rome appointed a magistrate to judge disputes involving foreigners, developing a flexible body of law.

name: qin-legalist-code
description: China's Qin dynasty imposed a strict, uniform legal code with severe punishments across the unified empire.

name: trial-of-jesus
description: Roman governor Pontius Pilate tried Jesus of Nazareth in Jerusalem, ordering his crucifixion.

name: gaius-institutes-written
description: The jurist Gaius produced an influential Roman legal textbook used to train students for centuries.

name: digest-of-justinian
description: Justinian's jurists distilled centuries of Roman legal writing into an authoritative reference work.

name: lex-salica-issued
description: The Frankish kingdom codified its customs in the Salic Law, later cited to bar women from royal succession.

name: tang-code-promulgated
description: China's Tang dynasty issued a comprehensive penal code that influenced legal systems across East Asia.

name: visigothic-code
description: Spain's Visigoths issued a unified code applying the same laws to both Goths and Romans alike.

name: law-of-aethelberht
description: Kent's King Aethelberht issued the earliest known English law code, written in the Anglo-Saxon language.

name: trial-by-ordeal-spreads
description: Medieval Europe judged guilt by ordeals of fire or water, trusting divine intervention to reveal the truth.

name: gratian-decretum
description: Gratian compiled church laws into a systematic text that became the foundation of canon law study.

name: assize-of-clarendon
description: England's Henry II expanded royal courts and grand juries, advancing the English common law system.

name: sachsenspiegel-compiled
description: This German law book recorded customary law in the vernacular and shaped Central European justice for ages.

name: jury-trial-replaces-ordeal
description: After the church banned ordeals, English courts turned to juries of peers to decide guilt or innocence.

name: model-parliament-statutes
description: Edward I's English statute codified dozens of laws, becoming a cornerstone of medieval common law.

name: siete-partidas
description: Castile's Alfonso X created a sweeping legal code covering law, religion, and daily life in Spain.

name: great-yassa-code
description: Genghis Khan established the Yassa, a secret legal code governing the vast Mongol Empire's affairs.

name: magdeburg-rights-spread
description: This German town law granting self-governance spread to hundreds of cities across Central Europe.

name: statute-of-laborers
description: After the Black Death, England capped wages and forced laborers to work, sparking widespread resentment.

name: ming-code-issued
description: China's Ming dynasty issued a detailed legal code that governed the empire for nearly three centuries.

name: carolina-criminal-code
description: Charles V issued the first general criminal code of the Holy Roman Empire, standardizing trials and torture.

name: statute-of-artificers
description: England regulated apprenticeships, wages, and working hours, shaping labor law for over two centuries.

name: court-of-star-chamber-peak
description: England's secretive Star Chamber court tried opponents without juries, becoming a symbol of royal abuse.

name: trial-of-charles-i
description: England put its own king on trial for treason and executed him, an unprecedented act in European history.

name: petition-of-right
description: English Parliament forced King Charles I to accept limits on taxation and imprisonment without cause.

name: pendle-witch-trials
description: England tried and hanged ten people for witchcraft in Lancashire, one of the era's best-documented cases.

name: blackstone-commentaries
description: William Blackstone published an influential survey of English common law, widely read on both sides of the Atlantic.

name: beccaria-on-crimes
description: Cesare Beccaria condemned torture and the death penalty, founding modern criminal justice reform.

name: somerset-slavery-case
description: An English court ruled slavery unsupported by common law, freeing a slave and fueling abolitionism.

name: guillotine-first-used
description: France adopted the guillotine for executions, intended as a swift and equal method of capital punishment.

name: us-supreme-court-first-session
description: The newly created United States Supreme Court held its first session in New York City.

name: marbury-v-madison
description: The US Supreme Court established judicial review, its power to strike down unconstitutional laws.

name: first-modern-penitentiary
description: Philadelphia opened a prison built around solitary confinement, pioneering a new model of incarceration.

name: first-detective-bureau
description: London's Scotland Yard formed a plainclothes detective branch, an early model for modern police work.

name: fugitive-slave-act
description: A US law forced citizens to help capture escaped slaves, deepening sectional conflict before the Civil War.

name: fingerprinting-adopted
description: Argentine police solved a murder using fingerprints, launching their use in criminal investigations worldwide.

name: dreyfus-retrial-pardon
description: France pardoned officer Alfred Dreyfus after a notorious wrongful conviction split the nation.

name: pinkerton-detective-agency
description: Allan Pinkerton founded a private detective agency in Chicago that pursued criminals across the United States.

name: fbi-founded
description: The United States created a federal investigative bureau, later renamed the FBI, to enforce national laws.

name: lindbergh-kidnapping-trial
description: Dubbed the trial of the century, a German immigrant was convicted of kidnapping the aviator's baby son.

name: interpol-founded
description: Police agencies created an international body in Vienna to coordinate the fight against cross-border crime.

name: leopold-and-loeb-trial
description: Two wealthy Chicago students were tried for a thrill killing in a case that gripped the United States.

name: moscow-show-trials
description: Stalin staged public trials of Soviet officials on fabricated charges, executing rivals during the Great Purge.

name: tokyo-war-crimes-trials
description: An Allied tribunal tried Japanese leaders for wartime atrocities, the Pacific counterpart to Nuremberg.

name: universal-declaration-human-rights
description: The United Nations adopted a declaration setting out fundamental human rights for all people everywhere.

name: genocide-convention-adopted
description: The United Nations made genocide an international crime, pledging to prevent and punish it worldwide.

name: european-court-human-rights
description: Europe established a court in Strasbourg to hear cases of human rights violations against member states.

name: gideon-v-wainwright
description: The US Supreme Court ruled that poor defendants have a right to a free lawyer in criminal cases.

name: eichmann-trial
description: Israel tried Nazi organizer Adolf Eichmann in Jerusalem for his role in the Holocaust, then executed him.

name: death-penalty-abolished-uk
description: Britain suspended capital punishment for murder, ending centuries of executions for the crime.

name: amnesty-international-founded
description: A London lawyer launched a global movement to free prisoners of conscience and defend human rights.

name: pentagon-papers-ruling
description: The US Supreme Court allowed newspapers to publish secret Vietnam War documents, upholding press freedom.

name: truth-reconciliation-commission
description: South Africa's Truth and Reconciliation Commission opened hearings into apartheid-era crimes and abuses.

name: rwanda-tribunal-established
description: The United Nations created a court to prosecute those responsible for the Rwandan genocide.

name: oj-simpson-trial
description: A televised murder trial of the former football star gripped the United States before his acquittal.

name: international-criminal-court-opens
description: A permanent court opened in The Hague to prosecute genocide, war crimes, and crimes against humanity.

name: guantanamo-bay-opens
description: The United States began holding terrorism suspects at a military prison in Cuba, sparking legal disputes.

name: gdpr-takes-effect
description: The European Union's sweeping data privacy law took effect, reshaping how companies handle personal data.
